The smell of salt and diesel still clings to my old seabag, a phantom limb of those days. I can almost feel the rocking of the transport ship, the endless horizon blurring into a watercolor dream. Even now, decades later, the taste of the hardtack lingers, a bitter reminder of a young man's first taste of war.

I still remember the endless blue, the taste of salt spray on my lips, and the constant hum and vibration deep in my bones. Every sunrise felt like a brand new day, but every sunset brought a longing for solid ground. We were a city at sea, a steel island far from home.

I remember the peculiar way we were transported. Instead of the usual route through New Jersey to New York, we went directly from Fort Meade to a ferry dock. We boarded the Queen Elizabeth, a massive troop transport ship that had never been used for civilian service. With around 16,000 men on board, we were packed into bunks six deep. I was nervous about the journey, especially since we were told to wear life vests and stay within designated areas. As we sailed out of the harbor, I caught a glimpse of a towering figure draped in robes, holding a torch high above her head, illuminating the path for those seeking refuge and opportunity. It was a bittersweet reminder of home.

The landscape ahead was marked by rolling hills, quaint villages, and a rich history that felt palpable in the air, hinting at the battles that had shaped this land. In 1960, during the Cuban Missile Crisis, my brother, who was in the Air Force, encouraged me to see if I could transfer to the Air Force. I went to their base, and they welcomed me with open arms, provided I could pass the physical. I did, and I found my new role immensely enjoyable. As the base transportation officer at Bakalar Air Force Base in Columbus, Indiana, I was in charge of the motor pool, overseeing 22 mechanics and about 24 drivers. We provided taxi and bus services and ensured that all vehicles were operational. It was a fulfilling assignment, especially during a time when many of my drivers and mechanics were highly educated individuals who had joined the reserves to serve their country, often facing the moral dilemmas of military engagement and the societal pressures of a deeply divided nation. They were committed to supporting the war effort while seeking to balance their civilian lives, reflecting the complex realities of a generation caught in the turmoil of conflict and protest.

This wasn’t just any runway; it was made of aluminum panels, each about 2-by-8 feet, that locked together to create a runway stretching about a thousand feet long. It served as a reminder of how quickly things can change in the military and how often we move on to the next task before fully appreciating the last one.

But just as I was finishing my studies, World War II broke out, and I was asked to join a unit heading overseas. I remember boarding the ship, the Pastura, a former elegant cruise liner now repurposed for the war. We were crammed into staterooms meant for two, with eleven nurses sharing one. The journey across the ocean was nerve-wracking; we had to keep everything dark to avoid enemy submarines. We landed in Casablanca after eleven long days, and from there, we were transported by train to Mateur, where we set up our camp in what had once been a French military training facility.

One of my most memorable experiences in the Air Force occurred during our monthly war exercises. Living on a small, tropical island in the Pacific, surrounded by crystal-clear waters and lush vegetation, meant we regularly faced the threat of severe storms, including tornadoes and hurricanes. The humidity was often stifling, and the vibrant sunsets were a stark contrast to the dark clouds that could roll in unexpectedly. Despite the weather warnings, we had to volunteer to move logistical supplies outside to the flight line where the planes were parked. I could have ended up in a facility in San Antonio, surrounded by fellow service members, or even overseas in England, living in barracks and following a strict routine. I spent my formative years here, graduating in 1967. Those times were filled with the laughter of friends during spirited lunch breaks and the excitement of Friday night games under the bright lights. The hallways echoed with chatter and the sound of lockers slamming, while classrooms buzzed with discussions and the thrill of learning. But as I reflect, I remember the atmosphere around town regarding the service. It was a period when patriotism ran deep. While the rest of the country was grappling with protests and demonstrations, our little town stood apart. People here were proud, and I shared that pride. I always believed it was our duty to help our country, and that sentiment was palpable in the air. It was a different world in New Carlisle, one where the call to serve was met with respect and honor.

We were out on an outpost when the MPs arrived, and I immediately felt a sense of unease. They were looking for a young soldier, and I knew that MPs don’t come to the front lines unless it’s serious. When they called out his name, I held my breath, wondering if they would call mine next. Thankfully, they didn’t. They took him away, explaining that his mother had sent a letter stating he was underage. He protested, claiming she was lying, but they had official records with his name, date of birth, and other identifying details that confirmed his age. They took him off, leaving me behind, and I felt a mix of relief and concern.

I remember the day I decided to dedicate my life to helping others. As a child, I was always the one running around the neighborhood, rescuing fallen birds and tending to my friends when they scraped their knees. I found joy in caring for those in need, whether it was a simple cut or a little bird that required assistance. When the Great Depression hit and my father lost everything, my mother suggested a path that involved service and compassion. It seemed like a perfect fit, so we explored our options. The best institution in New York State at the time was Saint Francis Hospital in Poughkeepsie, which was a bit of a trek from our home on Long Island, but we had a summer house nearby. I applied, got accepted, and spent three wonderful years there, learning the art of healing and the science of care. I immersed myself in understanding the human body, mastering the skills of observation, and developing the ability to comfort those in distress. I learned to administer treatments, manage medications, and provide emotional support to patients and their families. Under the guidance of the black Franciscans, a highly educated order of nuns, I grew in my ability to advocate for those who were vulnerable and to bring hope to those in pain.

text: After graduating, I won a scholarship to Mount Sinai Hospital in New York City, where I specialized in neurology and neurosurgery. Just as I was completing my studies, a significant global conflict erupted, marked by widespread mobilization and a call to arms that swept across nations. I was called to join a unit heading overseas, where the atmosphere was charged with urgency and uncertainty. I remember boarding the ship, the Pastura, a former elegant cruise liner now repurposed for military purposes, its luxurious amenities stripped away to accommodate the needs of the war effort. We were crammed into staterooms meant for two, with eleven nurses sharing one, a reflection of the desperate circumstances. The journey across the ocean was nerve-wracking; we had to keep everything dark to avoid detection by enemy forces lurking beneath the waves. After eleven long days, filled with anxiety and anticipation, we landed in Casablanca, a strategic point in the conflict, and from there, we were transported by train to Mateur, where we set up our camp in what had once been a French military training facility, now repurposed for the ongoing struggle.

text: I was discharged at Camp Atterbury, Indiana, on November 12, 1945. When we first landed on the beach, our rations mainly consisted of powdered eggs and K rations. With a bit of the local language I had picked up in school, I ventured out one night and asked, "Avez-vous des œufs?" (Do you have eggs?). To my delight, I managed to get my hands on eight eggs, a small taste of the culinary delights that the region was known for.

text: At Camp Stewart, I trained on a large caliber anti-aircraft artillery system designed to intercept enemy aircraft. They selected me and a few others based on our intelligence test scores to work in the director section, where we operated a sophisticated Sperry gyroscope mechanical computer. This device calculated various factors like altitude and wind to help track fast-moving enemy planes and direct our fire with precision. After completing my training, I returned home for a week before being shipped to Camp Hahn, California, where I was assigned to another artillery battalion equipped with similar heavy weaponry. I remember the luxury of working in an air-conditioned trailer while others were outside in the elements, loading shells and preparing the guns for action. We were gearing up for deployment to the Pacific, and I thought how wonderful it would be to be stationed on tropical islands.

I had developed a spot of pneumonia during my last days in Vietnam, which I contracted while waiting in a humid, crowded area near the coast, surrounded by the sounds of military activity and the scent of saltwater. I had never had any health problems during my time there, but after sleeping on the ground for a couple of days, I ended up in the hospital at Fort Lewis.

We were loading up trucks for maneuvers in Grafenwoehr when the captain delivered the unexpected news: "The war in Europe is over, guys. We’re not going to maneuver." A wave of relief washed over us, mingled with disbelief and a sense of uncertainty about what lay ahead. The air was thick with a mix of joy and apprehension, as we realized that the long, grueling conflict had finally come to an end, leaving us to ponder the next chapter in a world forever changed.

Reflecting on World War II, I believe history books often overlook the profound impact the war had on everyday life. The sheer scale of involvement and sacrifice is difficult to convey through written accounts or films. As a young man, I remember feeling out of place as a civilian surrounded by uniforms. Rationing was a part of life—shoes, food, gasoline, and tires were all limited. Blackouts were common; the bustling metropolis, with its iconic skyline and vibrant streets, was dimmed to protect against enemy submarines. My uncle, a chef at a renowned restaurant perched high above the city, saw it close because they couldn't risk the lights revealing their location.

text: In 1968, my unit was called to go to Vietnam, and I was appointed as the officer in charge of the security police for a prominent air wing known for its strategic operations and vital support roles. We had everything prepared for deployment, and I had even arranged for my family to rotate visits while I was stationed at Wright-Patterson Air Force Base, a hub for advanced military logistics and training. However, the night before I was set to leave, I received a call from Colonel Holbert, who informed me that I wouldn’t need to go to active duty because we had enough security personnel already deployed. I was relieved and felt no bitterness about the situation; I understood how quickly things could change.

text: In 1949, when I was just 16 years old, finding work was a daunting task. Initially, we worked on the Bell Airacobra, a fighter plane that had earned the grim nickname "flying coffin" among pilots. The design had its flaws, especially with the engine placement, which created hazards in crashes. Fortunately, when we were deployed overseas during that pivotal summer of intense conflict, we didn’t encounter any P-39s in combat. Our journey across the Atlantic was tense, traveling in a convoy from New York Harbor, past the Statue of Liberty, and up to Halifax, Nova Scotia. The naval protection boats changed course every seven minutes to evade enemy submarines, and we spent 14 days crossing the North Atlantic before finally docking in Clyde, Scotland, amidst the backdrop of a world at war.

We were supposed to be the bulwark, holding the line against a tide that never seemed to stop coming. Sometimes, late at night, the weight of that responsibility felt like it would crush you flat.

I remember the choppers always circling, kicking up dust devils on the landing strip. Some days, you’d just see them hauling mangled metal back, the silence broken only by the groan of the winch. We were always on standby, hoping we wouldn't get the call, but knowing we probably would.

The smell of jasmine always takes me back. We were so young, convinced we were doing the right thing, halfway across the world. Came home to a country that didn't understand, didn't want to understand.
